title: Beyond linearity
text:
Piecewise linear regression
Instead of fitting a high-degree polynomial over the entire range of \(X\), piecewise polynomial regression involves fitting separate low-degree polynomials over different regions of \(X\). For example, a piecewise cubic polynomial works by fitting a cubic regression model of the form
\[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2x_i^2+\beta_3x_i^3+\epsilon_i,\]
where the coefficients differ in different parts of the range of \(X\). The points where the coefficients change are called knots \(\xi_k\), \(k=1,\dots,K\).
Let us begin with a piecewise linear (degree=1):

pred1 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age<5,]))
pred2 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age >=5 & triceps$age<10,]))
pred3 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=10 & triceps$age<20,]))
pred4 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=20 & triceps$age<30,]))
pred5 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=30 & triceps$age<40,]))
pred6 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=40,]))
tri.age.plot + 
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age<5,], 
            aes(y = pred1, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age >=5 & triceps$age<10,], 
            aes(y = pred2, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=10 & triceps$age<20,], 
            aes(y = pred3, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=20 & triceps$age<30,], 
            aes(y = pred4, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=30 & triceps$age<40,], 
            aes(y = pred5, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=40,], 
            aes(y = pred6, x=age), size = 1, col="blue")







Continuous piecewise linear regression
We want a continuous function. Let us define a truncated power basis function (here of degree=1) per knot \(\xi\),
\[h(x, \xi)=(x-\xi)^1_{+}=
\begin{cases}
  x-\xi, \text{\,if\,} x>\xi\\
  0, \text{\,else}.
\end{cases}
\]
The continuous piecewise regression equation is
\[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2 h(x_i,5)+\cdots+\beta_6 h(x_i,40) + \epsilon_i\]
This can be done -by hand- or with B-splines splines::bs()

pred7 <- predict(lm(triceps~ age + I((age-5)*(age>=5)) +
                                   I((age-10)*(age >= 10)) +
                                   I((age-20)*(age >= 20)) +
                                   I((age-30)*(age >= 30)) +
                                  I((age-40)*(age >= 40)),
                    data = triceps))
library(splines)
pred.lm.bs <- predict(lm(triceps ~ bs(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40),degree=1), data=triceps))
tri.age.plot +
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred.lm.bs, x=age), size = 1, col="blue")+
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred7+.2, x=age), size = 1, col="red")

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Splines
Quadratic spline
With
\[h(x, \xi)=(x-\xi)_{+}^2=
\begin{cases}
  (x-\xi)^2, \text{\,if\,} x>\xi\\
  0, \text{\,else},
\end{cases}
\]
we have as regression equation \[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2x_i^2+\beta_3 h(x_i,5)+\cdots+\beta_7 h(x_i,40) + \epsilon_i\]

pred.quadsmooth <- predict(lm(triceps~ age + I(age^2) + 
                    I((age-5)^2*(age>=5)) +
                    I((age-10)^2*(age>=10)) +
                    I((age-20)^2*(age>=20)) +
                    I((age-30)^2*(age>=30)) +
                    I((age-40)^2*(age>=40)),
                    data = triceps))
pred.quadsmooth2 <- predict(lm(triceps ~ bs(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40),degree=2), data=triceps))                        
tri.age.plot +
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred.quadsmooth, x=age), size = 1, col="blue")+
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred.quadsmooth2+.2, x=age), size = 1, col="red")







Cubic Spline
Most often, cubic splines are used. Adding the following truncated power basis function per knot,
\[h(x, \xi)=(x-\xi)_{+}^3=
\begin{cases}
  (x-\xi)^3, \text{\,if\,} x>\xi\\
  0, \text{\,else},
\end{cases}
\]
to the model for a cubic polynomial will lead to a discontinuity in only the third derivative at \(\xi\); the function will remain continuous, with continuous first and second derivatives, at each of the knots:
\[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2x_i^2+\beta_3x_i^3+\beta_4h(x_i,5)+\cdots+\beta_8h(x_i,40) + \epsilon_i\]
One can show that a cubic spline has \(K+4\) parameters.
Natural Spline
Natural splines have an additional restriction that the fitted curve linear at the extremes, splines::ns()

cub.splines.bs <- lm(triceps ~ bs(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), data=triceps)
cub.splines.ns <- lm(triceps ~ ns(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), data=triceps)


tri.age.plot <- ggplot(triceps, aes(x=age, y=triceps)) +
                 geom_point(alpha=0.55, color="black") + 
                 theme_minimal() 
tri.age.plot +
    stat_smooth(method = "lm", 
               formula = y~bs(x,knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), 
               lty = 1, col = "green") + 
    stat_smooth(method = "lm", 
               formula = y~ns(x,knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), 
               lty = 1, col = "red")


polynomial cubic splines (green), natural cubic splines (red)

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Smoothing splines
Avoids the knot selection problem completely by using a maximal set of knots. The complexity of the fit is controlled by regularization. Problem: among all functions \(f(x)\) with two continuous derivatives, find one that minimizes the penalized residual sum of squares
\[
  RSS(f,\lambda)=\sum_{i=1}^N(y_i-f(x_i))^2+\lambda[f''(t)]^2dt
\tag{1}\]
where \(\lambda\) is a fixed smoothing parameter. The first term measures closeness to the data, while the second term penalizes curvature in the function, and \(\lambda\) establishes a tradeoff between the two. Special cases: \(\lambda=0\) (no constraint on \(f\)) and \(\lambda=\infty\) (\(f\) has to be linear).
The function \(f(x)\) that minimizes (Equation 1) can be shown to have some special properties: it is a piecewise cubic polynomial with knots at the unique values of \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) and continuous first and second derivatives at each knot. Furthermore, it is linear in the region outside of the extreme knots. In other words, the function \(f(x)\) that minimizes (Equation 1) is a natural cubic spline with knots at \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) ! However, it is not the same natural cubic spline that one would get if one applied the basis function approach described above – rather, it is a shrunken version of such a natural cubic spline, where the value of the tuning parameter \(\lambda\) in (Equation 1) controls the level of shrinkage.
Smoothing splines are implemented in smooth.spline().

\(\lambda\) determined with cross-validation

sspline <- smooth.spline(x=triceps$age, y=triceps$triceps, cv=TRUE)

Warning in smooth.spline(x = triceps$age, y = triceps$triceps, cv = TRUE): cross-validation with non-unique 'x' values seems doubtful

plot(triceps$age, triceps$triceps)
lines(sspline, lwd=3,col="red")







The extremes: no smooth and max smooth

ssplineNosmooth <- smooth.spline(x=triceps$age, y=triceps$triceps, lambda=0)
ssplineMaxsmooth <- smooth.spline(x=triceps$age, y=triceps$triceps, lambda=100)
plot(triceps$age, triceps$triceps)
lines(ssplineNosmooth, lwd=2,col="red")
lines(ssplineMaxsmooth, lwd=2,col="blue")

title: Design Matrix for interaction effects
text:
Problem discussed with Niklaus Meier (Thank you.)
Assume we want interaction terms of continuous variables (variables in \(\mathbf{X}\)) with categorical variables (variables in \(\mathbf{J}\)). The corresponding Design Matrix is the Transposed Khatri-Rao Product; this is a row-by-row Kronecker product of two matrices.
\[
\mathbf{J} \bullet \mathbf{X}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{J}[1,] \otimes \mathbf{X}[1,]\\\hline
\mathbf{J}[2,] \otimes \mathbf{X}[2,]\\\hline
\mathbf{J}[3,] \otimes \mathbf{X}[3,]\\\hline
...\\\hline
\mathbf{J}[n,] \otimes \mathbf{X}[n,]\\
\end{bmatrix}
\]

title: Noncollapsibility of the odds ratio
text:
Assume having a binary outcome \(Y\) and a treatment indicator \(X\) denoting if patient was randomised to treatment (\(X=1\)) or control (\(X=0\)). The analysis would be to fit a simple logistic regression model
\[
P(Y=1|X)=\text{expit}(\alpha_0+\alpha_1X),
\]
with \(\text{expit}(\alpha_0+\alpha_1X)=\frac{\exp(\alpha_0+\alpha_1X)}{1+\exp(\alpha_0+\alpha_1X)}\).
Often, we write the model with the linear predictor, that is, the logit transformed expectation,
\[
\boxed{\text{logit}P(Y=1|X)=\alpha_0+{\color{red}\alpha_1}X}.
\]
Of course, there will be other factors that influence the probability that \(Y=1\). Assume another variable \(C\) which influences the probability that \(Y=1\). The conditional model would be
\[
\boxed{\text{logit}P(Y=1|X,C)=\beta_0+{\color{red}\beta_1}X+\beta_2C}.
\]
Now, under randomisation, \(C\) and \(X\) are (population) independent – that is – there is no confounding of the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) by \(C\) in place.
Though \(C\) is not a confounder, in general, \({\color{red}\alpha_1 \neq \beta_1}\). The parameters (log odds ratios) in the marginal and conditional model are different. The proof is based on William’s Tower rule for conditional expectations:
\[
\begin{align}
E(Y|X)=&E(E(Y|X,C)|X)\\
=&E(\text{expit}(\beta_0+\beta_1X+\beta_2C)|X)\\
\neq &\text{expit}(\beta_0+\beta_1X+\beta_2E(C|X))
\end{align}
\]







With other link functions (in log-linear models) and identity (in linear models)), however, the two quantities are equal.

title: Noncollapsibility of the odds ratio
text:
Marginal model

equatiomatic::extract_eq(modM)

\[
\log\left[ \frac { P( \operatorname{Ydich} = \operatorname{1} ) }{ 1 - P( \operatorname{Ydich} = \operatorname{1} ) } \right] = \alpha + \beta_{1}(\operatorname{X})
\]

title: Noncollapsibility of the odds ratio
text:
Conditional model

equatiomatic::extract_eq(modC)

\[
\log\left[ \frac { P( \operatorname{Ydich} = \operatorname{1} ) }{ 1 - P( \operatorname{Ydich} = \operatorname{1} ) } \right] = \alpha + \beta_{1}(\operatorname{X}) + \beta_{2}(\operatorname{C})
\]

predgB<-predict(modC,newdata=data.frame(C=C[X==1],X=1),se.fit=TRUE,type="response")

title: Compositions
text:
Sample space of compositional data
The sample space of compositional data with \(D\) parts is a simplex \(\mathcal{S}^D\) of dimension \(D-1\):
\(\mathcal{S}^D=\{\mathbf{x}=[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_D]\in \mathbb{R}^D \mid
x_i>0, i=1,2,\dots,D; \sum_{i=1}^Dx_i=\mathcal{k}\}\)
The only information is given by the ratios between components, so the information of a composition is preserved under multiplication by any positive constant. Therefore, the sample space of compositional data can always be assumed to be a standard simplex, i.e. \(\mathcal{k}
= 1\).
Normalization to the standard simplex is called closure and is
\(\mathcal{C}[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_D]=\left[\frac{x_1}{\sum_{i=1}^D x_i},\frac{x_2}{\sum_{i=1}^D x_i}, \dots,\frac{x_D}{\sum_{i=1}^D x_i}\right]\)
Example
4 Raters rated a 4-part-composition on \(n=20\) subjects. See the interactive Tetrahedron

title: Aber der Kaiser hat ja gar nichts an!
text:
In Zeiten, in denen – an vom Steuerzahler finanzierten Hochschulen – Sprachleitfaden verteilt werden, scheint es doch wichtig, Gegendruck auch aus der politischen Mitte – also nicht nur von der extremen Rechten – aufzubauen. Es ist nicht die Aufgabe staatlicher Institutionen, an der Verbreitung von Ideologien mitzuwirken und Vorgaben zu machen, was gute Sprache ist. Sprachpolitik zu betreiben ist aus meiner Sicht etwas vom Schlimmsten, was eine Hochschule machen kann.
Das grammatikalische bildet nicht das biologische Geschlecht ab. Diese einfache Tatsache wird von der Linguistik der Genderist*innen geleugnet. Wer sich z.B. in der Klimadebatte gleich verhielte wie die Gender-Aktivisten mit der Sprache, würde sofort (und zurecht) als Wissenschaftsleugner diffamiert. Auch sind die vorgebrachten Assoziationsstudien gegen das generische Maskulinum wenig überzeugend:

Um die mangelnde Eignung des generischen Maskulinums zum inklusiven Formulieren vorzuführen, wird immer wieder gerne folgende Frage präsentiert: „Wer ist dein Lieblingsschauspieler?“ (Stahlberg et al., 2001). Werden auf diese Frage überwiegend männliche Schauspieler genannt, so werten Befürworter des Genderns dies als Beleg dafür, dass generische Maskulina vorrangig die Vorstellung von Männern auslösen. Allerdings ist diese Frage nicht im Geringsten geeignet, die Untauglichkeit des inklusiven Maskulinums zu demonstrieren. Denn diese Frage ist keine typische Verwendung des generischen Maskulinums. Würde man ganz allgemein nach Lieblingsschauspielern fragen, so lautete die korrekte Formulierung „Wer/welche sind deine Lieblingsschauspieler?“ (Frage im Plural!). Im Singular würde man entweder fragen „Wer ist deine Lieblingsschauspielerin?“ oder „Wer ist dein männlicher Lieblingsschauspieler?“ Das generische Maskulinum wird bei allgemeinen Aussagen verwendet – nicht, wenn konkrete Personen gemeint sind (wie in der Frage nach dem Lieblingsschauspieler).

Letztendlich ist es bei vielen die Angst, als frauenfeindlich (oder feindlich anderen sozialen Geschlechtern gegenüber) dazustehen, die es überhaupt ermöglicht, dass solche Sprachpolitik an den Hochschulen betrieben werden kann:

Vielleicht endet die Geschichte des geschlechtergerechten Sprachumbaus mit all seinen grotesken Auswüchsen eines Tages wie Andersens Märchen vom Kaiser und seinen neuen Kleidern. Dort ist es bekanntlich ein Kind, das am Ende ausruft: „Aber der Kaiser hat ja gar nichts an!“ und dem Spuk damit ein Ende macht. Zuvor wollte in dem Märchen niemand eingestehen, dass er überhaupt keine Kleider sieht, weil er Angst hatte, in diesem Fall für dumm zu gelten. Beim Gendern ist es unsere Angst, von den anderen für „frauenfeindlich“ gehalten zu werden oder nicht auf der Höhe der Zeit zu sein, die viele mitlaufen lässt. Dabei ist die geschlechtergerechte Sprache noch nicht einmal ein „neues Kleid“, sondern ein über 40 Jahre altes Konzept, bei dem die Frage erlaubt sein muss, was es heute noch für ein gleichberechtigtes Miteinander der Geschlechter zu leisten vermag.

Für Tomas Kubelik ist die Sprache unschuldig. Hier ein unterhaltsamer, humorvoller und gut aufgebauter Vortrag. Sein aus meiner Sicht nachvollziehbares Fazit ist:

Alle Formen des Genderns sind unbrauchbar. Sie sind in sich widersprüchlich, sie sind nicht konsequent durchführbar, sie stossen an die Grenzen von Logik, Praktikabilität und Akzeptanz. Sie sind unästhetisch, unökonomisch und irreführend. (Tomas Kubelik, Vortrag und Text)

Für Ingo Meyer (Das Märchen vom Gendersterntaler) ist das Hauptargument gegen das Gendern:

Sprache entwickelt sich seit Jahrhunderten. Was funktioniert, setzt sich durch; was die Verständigung erschwert, wird abgeschliffen. Nie ist es ohne Schaden gelungen, diesen unbewussten Akt nachzuahmen. Zwar stimmt es: Wenn ich die Meldung „Sonntagsausflügler drängten ins Grüne“ lese, stelle ich mir die Menschen derzeit vorwiegend weiß vor. Um diesen Satz für mich „gerechter“ zu machen, bräuchte ich also vielerlei Hinweise. Gerecht in diesem Sinne wäre eine Formulierung wie „Die LSBTI+, PoC, alte und junge Menschen inkludierenden Sonntagsausflügler*innen drängten ins Grüne“. Das ist offensichtlich absurd. Sprache hat nicht die Aufgabe, von Dritten erwünschte Bedeutungen in unsere Köpfe zu pflanzen. Es gibt keine geschlechtergerechte Sprache. Es gibt überhaupt keine gerechte Sprache. Es steht uns aber frei, die vorhandene Sprache gerecht zu verwenden.

Ein einfach und klar geschriebenes Buch ist auch: Fabian Payr: Von Menschen und Mensch*innen. 20 gute Gründe, mit dem Gendern aufzuhören:

„Studentin kann nur deshalb ‚weibliche Person, die studiert‘ bedeuten, weil das zugrunde liegende Wort Student ‚Person, die studiert‘ bedeutet und nicht etwa ‚männliche Person, die studiert‘“ (Lieb & Richter, 1990, S. 150).

Natürlich lehnen auch sehr viele Frauen das Gendern ab. Persönlich kenne ich keine einzige Frau in meinem nicht-akademischen Umfeld, die Gendern gut findet.

Ich bin eine Frau und ich fühle mich belästigt von den In-Endungen, von dem Binnen-I und dem ganzen syntaktischen Gleichberechtigungsgefummel. Die fast extremistischen Züge des Verweiblichungswahns von Sprache haben wohl mit der Tradition des Alice Schwarzer Opferfeminismus zu tun. Offenbar haben bis heute manche mental nicht aus der weiblichen Opferrolle herausgefunden. Als Therapie und als Beleg des weiblichen Selbstbewusstseins fordere ich das generische Maskulinum daher zurück. Ich mache auch gleich ernst damit und verabschiede mich an dieser Stelle im maskulinen Gestus von Ihnen, lieber Leser, liebe Bürger, liebe Staatssekretäre, liebe Feministinnen, liebe Kritiker. Das fühlt sich gut an. (Dagmar Rosenfeld, in der Wochenzeitung die Zeit (2014) in einem „Aufschrei“)

Inzwischen wird auch in Frage gestellt, dass das biologische Geschlecht zweiwertig ist, die Biologie an sich wird als Naturwissenschaft nicht mehr ernst genommen. Ich hätte natürlich nie gedacht, dass ich mich eines Tages dafür rechtfertigen muss, im Statistik-Unterricht das biologische Geschlecht als Beispiel für eine zweiwertige kategoriale Variable heranzuziehen. Dazu ein Artikel aus unverdächtiger Quelle: Viele Geschlechter? Das ist Unfug!
Ob die extremen Anstrengungen für die so genannte Gleichstellung am Ende wirklich zielführend sind, wage ich doch sehr stark zu bezweifeln. So haben z.B. Länder mit hohem Gleichstellungsaufwand eine tieferen Frauen-Anteil an MINT Hochschul-Abschlüssen als Länder, die doch eher nicht als sehr matriarchalisch gelten. Trotz massiven Anstrengungen drängen Frauen in Skandinavien wieder mehr in klassische Frauenberufe. Das sollte uns zu denken geben.


Gender-Equality Paradox in Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics. See Stoet and Geary, 2018. See also corrigendum, 2019, and reply to Richardson et al., 2020.



In Frankreich hat die Académie française erklärt, das Gendern sei zur Verfolgung der damit bezweckten gesellschaftspolitischen Ziele (Gleichstellung) untauglich und kontraproduktiv. Der französische Bildungsminister hat die Verwendung geschlechtsneutraler Schriftsprache an Schulen per Erlass verboten.
Viele lesenswerte Texte zum Thema findet man unter Gendersterntaler und LinguistikUndGendern.

title: “All Is Integration”
text:
Statistics, viewed from a sufficient distance, is often presented as a science of estimation, testing, prediction, or uncertainty quantification. Yet beneath these familiar activities lies a deeper and more universal mathematical structure: the repeated necessity of integrating over what cannot be directly observed.












Model class
Example model
Observation model
What is integrated over?
Integral / summation structure
Philosophical interpretation
Additional explanation




Deterministic regression
\(y=x^\top\beta\)
none
nothing
none
Pure functional law
No probability model; data assumed to follow an exact deterministic relation.


Gaussian LM
\(Y_i=x_i^\top\beta+\varepsilon_i\)
\(\varepsilon_i\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\)
Gaussian error noise
implicit in Gaussian density
Additive uncertainty around deterministic mean
Classical regression with homoscedastic additive residuals.


One-sample \(t\)-test
\(Y_i\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\)
Gaussian
error variability
product of normal densities
Mean inference under stochastic sampling
Special case of Gaussian likelihood with one mean parameter.


Two-sample \(t\)-test
\(Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1G_i+\varepsilon_i\)
Gaussian
residual noise
Gaussian likelihood
Group differences with uncertainty
Equivalent to a linear model with a binary group predictor.


ANOVA
\(Y_{ij}=\mu+\alpha_j+\varepsilon_{ij}\)
Gaussian
residual noise
Gaussian likelihood
Group means plus stochastic variation
Multi-group extension of the linear model.


Logistic regression
\(Y_i\sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p_i)\)
Bernoulli
binary outcome randomness
product of Bernoulli probabilities
Uncertainty in binary realizations
Models probabilities through a link function such as the logit.


Poisson regression
\(Y_i\sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\mu_i)\)
Poisson
count randomness
product of Poisson probabilities
Event generation process
Variance equals the mean under the Poisson assumption.


Gamma GLM
\(Y_i\sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\mu_i,\phi)\)
Gamma
multiplicative positive noise
Gamma likelihood
Relative / proportional variability
Suitable for positive skewed outcomes with variance increasing quadratically with the mean.


Negative-binomial GLM
\(Y_i\sim NB(\mu_i,\theta)\)
NB
Poisson intensity heterogeneity
Poisson-Gamma mixture
Extra latent variability in rates
Can be derived as a Poisson model with a Gamma-distributed latent rate.


Probit regression
\(Y_i=1[Z_i>0]\)
latent Gaussian threshold
latent \(Z_i\)
\(\int 1(z>0)\phi(z)\,dz\)
Hidden continuous propensity
Binary outcomes arise from an unobserved continuous latent variable.


Finite mixture model
\(Y_i\sim \sum_k \pi_k f_k(y)\)
mixture
latent class membership
\(\sum_k \pi_k f_k(y)\)
Population heterogeneity
Data assumed to arise from several overlapping subpopulations.


Latent class analysis
categorical latent groups
multinomial
latent class \(C_i\)
\(\sum_c P(Y\mid C=c)P(C=c)\)
Hidden subpopulations
Common in psychometrics and sociology for identifying hidden categories.


Factor analysis
\(Y=\Lambda f+\varepsilon\)
Gaussian
latent factors \(f\)
\(\int f(y\mid f)p(f)df\)
Hidden dimensions generate observed variables
Correlations among variables explained through lower-dimensional latent traits.


IRT / Rasch
\(P(Y_{ij}=1)=\mathrm{logit}^{-1}(\theta_i-b_j)\)
Bernoulli
latent ability \(\theta_i\)
\(\int P(Y\mid\theta)p(\theta)d\theta\)
Hidden trait generates responses
Item responses modeled via latent ability and item difficulty.


Random intercept LMM
\(Y_{ij}=x_{ij}^\top\beta+b_j+\varepsilon_{ij}\)
Gaussian
random intercepts \(b_j\)
\(\int f(y\mid b)f(b)db\)
Cluster heterogeneity
Observations within a cluster become correlated through shared latent effects.


Random slope model
\(Y_{ij}=x_{ij}^\top\beta+b_{1j}+b_{2j}x_{ij}+\varepsilon\)
Gaussian
random coefficient vector
multivariate integral
Individual-specific effects
Allows regression slopes to vary between clusters or persons.


Logistic GLMM
binary mixed model
Bernoulli
random effects

Extends logistic regression to clustered or longitudinal data.

count mixed model

latent cluster intensity

Gamma

frailty term

latent states \(S_t\)

hidden states
\(\sum_{S_1,\dots,S_T}\)

Observed sequences generated from unobserved evolving states.

latent states

\(f(x)\sim GP\)

priors + random effects

parameters and random effects

Simultaneously integrates over latent effects and parameter uncertainty.


Posterior predictive distribution
\(p(y^{new}\mid y)\)

Bayesian prediction averages over all plausible parameter values.

\(p(y)\)

Central quantity in Bayesian model comparison and Bayes factors.

latent missing data

Alternates between estimating latent variables and parameters.


Missing-data model

missing values

Missing values treated as latent unknown quantities.

potential outcomes

title: The Magic of Linear Algebra
text:
Quantum Mechanics Through the Lens of Statistics
A modern perspective views the superposition principle as the fundamental empirical observation underlying quantum mechanics. Experiments indicate that if two states \(\psi_1\) and \(\psi_2\) are physically possible, then any linear combination
\[
a\psi_1 + b\psi_2
\]
is also a possible state. This implies that quantum states form a vector space, more precisely a Hilbert space. Once states are represented as vectors, the mathematical framework of linear algebra emerges naturally.
To be compatible with superposition, physical observables such as energy, momentum, or spin are represented by linear operators acting on the state space. Likewise, the time evolution of a quantum system must preserve superpositions and is therefore described by a linear dynamical law. The Schrödinger equation
\[
i\hbar \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial t}
=
H\psi
\]
can be understood as the mathematical expression of continuous linear time evolution, where \(H\) denotes the Hamiltonian, the operator associated with energy.
From a statistical perspective, there are several striking analogies. In multivariate statistics, a covariance matrix \(\Sigma\) summarizes the dependence structure among variables. In quantum mechanics, a Hermitian operator such as the Hamiltonian \(H\) plays a similarly central role by determining the structure of a physical system and its possible energy states.
Eigenvalue decompositions are fundamental in both fields. In principal component analysis (PCA),
\[
\Sigma v = \lambda v,
\]
where \(\lambda\) denotes the variance explained by the principal component \(v\). In quantum mechanics,
\[
H\psi = E\psi,
\]
where \(E\) denotes an energy level and \(\psi\) the corresponding energy eigenstate. In both cases, eigenvectors identify privileged directions or states, and eigenvalues quantify important properties of those directions.
There is also a conceptual analogy between latent variable models and quantum theory. In factor analysis, observed correlations are explained through unobserved latent factors:
\[
\Sigma = \Lambda \Phi \Lambda^\top + \Psi.
\]
Similarly, quantum mechanics postulates an underlying state \(\psi\) that is not directly observable. What can be observed are the probabilities of measurement outcomes, which are derived from the state through the Born rule:
\[
P(i)
=
\left|
\langle i \mid \psi \rangle
\right|^2.
\]
Thus, both statistics and quantum mechanics infer hidden structure from observable data.
Another parallel arises from spectral decomposition. In statistics, a covariance matrix can be decomposed as
\[
\Sigma = Q D Q^\top,
\]
where the columns of \(Q\) are eigenvectors and the diagonal elements of \(D\) are eigenvalues. In quantum mechanics, a Hermitian operator admits an analogous decomposition:
\[
H = U D U^\dagger,
\]
where \(U\) is a unitary matrix and \(D\) contains the energy eigenvalues. The mathematical structure is nearly identical; only the interpretation differs.
From this viewpoint, much of the mathematical machinery of quantum mechanics—Hilbert spaces, Hermitian operators, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, traces, and spectral decompositions—can be seen as consequences of the experimentally observed validity of superposition. The deepest mystery is perhaps not why quantum mechanics relies so heavily on linear algebra, but why nature itself appears to obey the superposition principle with such extraordinary precision.


Quantum Mechanics as Generalized Probability Theory
The previous discussion emphasized why quantum mechanics naturally leads to linear algebra. However, quantum theory can also be viewed as a generalization of probability theory. This perspective becomes particularly clear through the concept of the density matrix, which plays a role analogous to a probability distribution in classical statistics.
In classical probability theory, a random variable \(X\) takes values \(x_i\) with probabilities \(p_i\). The expectation of \(X\) is given by
\[
E(X)
=
\sum_i x_i p_i.
\]
The probability distribution contains all information necessary to predict future observations and compute expectations.
Quantum mechanics has a remarkably similar structure. The state of a system is described by a density matrix \(\rho\), while a measurable quantity is represented by a Hermitian operator \(A\). In this analogy, the density matrix plays a role similar to a probability distribution, whereas observables generalize the notion of random variables.
The expectation of an observable is given by
\[
E(A)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\rho A).
\]
This formula is the quantum analogue of the classical expectation.
To understand why, consider the spectral decomposition of the observable:
\[
A
=
\sum_i a_i P_i,
\]
where \(a_i\) are the eigenvalues of \(A\) and \(P_i\) are the corresponding projection operators. The eigenvalues represent the possible outcomes of a measurement. Their probabilities are determined by the Born rule:
\[
P(a_i)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\rho P_i).
\]
The expectation of the observable therefore becomes
\[
E(A)
=
\sum_i a_i P(a_i).
\]
Substituting the Born rule yields
\[
E(A)
=
\sum_i a_i \operatorname{tr}(\rho P_i).
\]
Using the linearity of the trace,
\[
E(A)
=
\operatorname{tr}
\left(
\rho
\sum_i a_i P_i
\right)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\rho A).
\]
Thus, the familiar formula for expectation in statistics reappears in quantum mechanics in matrix form.
The analogy can be summarized as follows:







Statistics
Quantum Mechanics




Probability distribution \(P\)
Density matrix \(\rho\)


Random variable \(X\)
Observable \(A\)


Possible values \(x_i\)
Eigenvalues \(a_i\)


Probabilities \(P(X=x_i)\)
Born probabilities \(\operatorname{tr}(\rho P_i)\)


Expectation \(E(X)\)
Expectation \(\operatorname{tr}(\rho A)\)



From this perspective, quantum mechanics may be viewed as a form of non-commutative probability theory. In classical probability, random variables are ordinary functions and therefore commute:
\[
XY = YX.
\]
In quantum mechanics, observables are operators and generally do not commute:
\[
AB \neq BA.
\]
This seemingly small mathematical change has profound consequences. Non-commutativity leads to uncertainty relations, interference phenomena, and many of the characteristic features that distinguish quantum mechanics from classical probability theory.
From a mathematical perspective, one may therefore view quantum mechanics as a generalization of probability theory in which probability distributions are replaced by density matrices, random variables by Hermitian operators, and integration by the trace operator. The familiar concepts of probabilities, expectations, variances, and mixtures survive, but they are expressed in the language of linear operators rather than functions.

The trace operator also plays an important role in both fields. In multivariate statistics,
\[
\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma)
\]
equals the total variance of the variables. In quantum mechanics, expected values can be written in terms of traces. For a quantum state represented by a density matrix \(\rho\) and an observable \(A\),
\[
E(A)
=

\]

For a statistician, one of the most illuminating ways to understand quantum mechanics is through the concept of the density matrix. In many respects, the density matrix plays a role analogous to that of a probability distribution in classical statistics.
In classical probability theory, a random variable \(X\) takes values \(x_i\) with probabilities \(p_i\). The expectation of \(X\) is given by
\[
E(X)
=
\sum_i x_i p_i.
\]

Quantum mechanics has a remarkably similar structure. The state of a system is described by a density matrix \(\rho\), while a measurable quantity is represented by a Hermitian operator \(A\). The expected value of the observable is
\[
E(A)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\rho A).
\]
This formula is the quantum analogue of the classical expectation.
To see the connection, consider the spectral decomposition of the observable:
\[
A
=
\sum_i a_i P_i,
\]
where \(a_i\) are the possible measurement outcomes (the eigenvalues of \(A\)) and \(P_i\) are the corresponding projection operators. The probability of observing outcome \(a_i\) is given by the Born rule,
\[
P(a_i)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\rho P_i).
\]
Substituting this into the classical expectation formula yields
\[
E(A)
=
\sum_i a_i P(a_i)
=
\sum_i a_i \operatorname{tr}(\rho P_i).
\]
Using the linearity of the trace,
\[
E(A)
=
\operatorname{tr}
\left(
\rho
\sum_i a_i P_i
\right)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\rho A).
\]
Thus, the familiar formula for expectation in statistics reappears in quantum mechanics in matrix form.
The analogy can be summarized as follows:







Statistics
Quantum Mechanics




Probability distribution \(p(x)\)
Density matrix \(\rho\)


Random variable \(X\)
Observable \(A\)


Possible values \(x_i\)
Eigenvalues \(a_i\)


Probabilities \(p_i\)
Born probabilities \(\operatorname{tr}(\rho P_i)\)


Expectation \(E(X)=\sum_i x_i p_i\)
Expectation \(E(A)=\operatorname{tr}(\rho A)\)



From this perspective, the density matrix can be viewed as a generalized probability distribution and the trace as a generalized form of integration. Classical statistics computes expectations by integrating a random variable with respect to a probability distribution. Quantum mechanics computes expectations by taking the trace of an observable with respect to a density matrix. The mathematical language differs, but the underlying idea is strikingly similar: both theories describe uncertainty through a state representation from which probabilities and expectations are derived.
One may therefore view quantum mechanics as a kind of non-commutative probability theory. In classical statistics, uncertainty is represented by probability measures and expectations are computed through sums or integrals. In quantum mechanics, uncertainty is represented by density matrices and expectations are computed through traces. The density matrix \(\rho\) plays a role analogous to a probability distribution, while Hermitian operators generalize the notion of random variables.
